title: Driving the Innovation Agenda in Uncertain Times. A Perspective from CEOs
gist: BCG argues that maintaining innovation investment through downturns is a durable source of outperformance, citing data that top innovators beat the market by 2.4pp annually and that down-market deals and crisis-era transformations generate superior shareholder returns. It also warns CEOs not to let AI crowd out a holistic innovation agenda.

insights.claim: Companies on BCG's Most Innovative Companies list (since 2005) outperformed the broader market by 2.4 percentage points annually on average, with the strongest gains during the 2008 downturn and the COVID-19 pandemic.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Supports overweighting persistent innovators going into stress periods rather than rotating to defensives that cut R&D.
insights.quote: Top innovators outperformed the broader market by 2.4 percentage points annually on average, with particularly strong gains during the 2008 financial downturn and the COVID-19 pandemic.

insights.claim: BCG found strategic M&A deals made in down markets generate 9 percentage points greater relative shareholder returns over the following two years than deals made in up markets.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Argues for accumulating/acquiring during drawdowns; flags acquirers who deploy capital in down markets as candidates for outperformance.
insights.quote: strategic deals made in down markets generate 9 percentage points greater relative shareholder returns over the following two years than deals made in up markets.

insights.claim: 43% of corporate transformation programs launched during the COVID-19 crisis achieved value-accretive growth, versus only 28% launched in years of comparative economic stability.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Crisis-timed transformations have a materially higher hit rate, reinforcing that volatility is a window, not just a threat.
insights.quote: 43% of transformation programs launched during the pandemic—certainly a moment of crisis—achieved value-accretive growth. That compares with only 28% launched in years of comparative global economic stability.

insights.claim: Volatility increases the need for innovation but tends to make organizations more risk-averse, pushing them to extract incremental growth from existing portfolios and underinvest in mid-to-long-term innovation.
insights.speaker: Judith Wallenstein
insights.so_what: Identifies a predictable behavioral trap that creates relative-value opportunities in firms that resist it.
insights.quote: While volatility actually increases the need for innovation, it often makes organizations more risk averse and paralyzes them.

themes.theme: AI should not be the whole innovation agenda

insights.claim: BCG cautions that while AI investment is essential to every company's innovation agenda, it should not make up all of it, and CEOs must align boards on a holistic set of innovation priorities.
insights.speaker: Judith Wallenstein
insights.so_what: Implies firms over-rotating capex purely into AI may underinvest in product/R&D edge; watch for balance in capital allocation narratives.
insights.quote: And while AI investments are an essential part of every company’s innovation agenda, they should not necessarily make up all of it

insights.claim: BCG frames AI as a tool to compress the innovation cycle—distilling unstructured data to spot market trends, running cheap virtual product simulations, and providing post-launch real-time feedback for product modifications.
insights.speaker: BCG Henderson Institute
insights.so_what: Points to where AI shows up as a cost/speed lever in R&D-heavy sectors rather than a standalone product.
